title: The hash you won't use
checkLabels.t4: You didn't use style on the tag
checkLabels.t12: The heading is still dark blue
why.empty: The editor is empty. Bring the files back and start again.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was. The same look is still written twice.
hint2.untouched: You work in both files. The class is added in `index.html`, the spare rule is deleted from `styles.css`.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed, or a quote is missing.
hint2.not-closed: `id` and `class` values go in quotes. One to open, one to close.
why.unlinked: The page can't find `styles.css`. You've edited the `link` line.
hint2.unlinked: Bring the files back and start again.
why.h1-broken: The heading isn't dark blue any more. You deleted the `h1` rule.
hint2.h1-broken: You're only deleting the id rule. The others stay as they are.
why.used-inline: You used `style` on the tag. You have a class — that's the tool.
why.lost-p: There aren't four paragraphs any more. You're adding a label, not deleting.
hint2.lost-p: All four sentences stay. The two notes get a `class`.
why.no-anchor: You deleted `id="hours"`. The link at the top points there — without it, it leads nowhere.
hint2.no-anchor: The id stays. It isn't for the colour, it's for the anchor.
hint3.no-anchor: `<a href="#hours">` looks for an element with `id="hours"`. Remove it and clicking does nothing. That's exactly what id is for.
hint4.no-anchor: Put `id="hours"` back on the opening-hours paragraph. Add a `class` to it as well — both live in the same tag.
why.wrong-count: The class is on the wrong number of paragraphs. There are two notes.
hint2.wrong-count: Opening hours and Christmas — those are the two pieces of extra information. One of them already has it.
hint3.wrong-count:
One element can carry both an `id` and a class: `

`. They don't exclude each other.
hint4.wrong-count: Add `class="note"` to the opening-hours paragraph. The Christmas one already has it.
why.wrong-ones: The class is on the wrong paragraphs. The two notes are the opening hours and Christmas.
why.no-note: There's no element with the class `note` any more. You deleted it.
hint2.no-note: In HTML: `class="note"`. In CSS: `.note { }`. No dot in the first, a dot in the second.
why.note-not-gray: A note is left without colour. The check looks at both.
hint2.note-not-gray: The `.note` rule already exists. Don't rewrite it — put the class on the second paragraph.
hint3.note-not-gray: If you deleted `.note` instead of `#hours`, you've gone backwards. You're deleting the id rule, not the class one.
why.note-not-italic: The notes are grey, but not all of them are italic.
why.id-rule-left: The `#hours` rule is still in `styles.css`. It works — and that's exactly the problem.
hint2.id-rule-left: It does the same thing as `.note`, only for one element. Once that paragraph carries the class, the id rule is redundant.
hint3.id-rule-left: Two rules doing the same thing: tomorrow you decide the notes should be blue and change one. The other keeps painting the opening hours grey. Half a day of hunting. And since an id beats a class, the one you forgot is the one that wins.
blocks.1.text: There's a second way to attach a label. It's called `id` and it's written almost like a class — except in CSS you call it with a hash instead of a dot.
blocks.5.text: It works. It does exactly what you'd expect. And almost nobody uses it for styling — here's why.
blocks.7.text: `id` means "this one, the only one". Not two, not five. One name, one element, across the whole page. The browser won't stop you from using two — the code just becomes invalid and things start behaving oddly in places that have nothing to do with styling.
blocks.8.text: And the reverse holds too: one element carries exactly one `id`. Classes you can stack as much as you like — `class="note urgent small"` is ordinary. `id="a b"` isn't two ids, it's one with a strange name.
blocks.9.text: So every id rule applies to exactly one element. Tomorrow you want another note — the rule doesn't cover it. You write a second one. Then a third. You're back exactly where you started in lesson 08.
blocks.10.text: A class is the opposite: as many elements as you like, one rule.
blocks.12.text: `id` isn't useless. Its job is just a different one — it's an ADDRESS, not a label:
blocks.13.items.0: A link anchor: `<a href="#hours">` jumps to the element with `id="hours"`. A class can't do that.
blocks.13.items.1: It ties a `<label>` to its field in a form — you saw that in the HTML course.
blocks.13.items.2: JavaScript uses it to find that exact element.
blocks.14.text: And one more thing that gets a whole lesson of its own: an id rule beats a class rule, even if the class is written further down. That sounds convenient until it becomes the reason you can't work out why your style isn't applying. Lesson 50 is about that.
blocks.16.text: Look at `styles.css`. The same look is written twice — once as `.note` from last lesson, once as `#hours`, added afterwards. Put `class="note"` on the opening-hours paragraph too, then delete the `#hours { }` block. The `id="hours"` itself STAYS in the HTML — the link at the top uses it as an anchor.
blocks.19.items.0: `#` in CSS, `id="..."` in HTML. Written like a class, behaves differently.
blocks.19.items.1: One id per page, one id per element. Classes have neither limit.
blocks.19.items.2: For styling — a class. For an address, an anchor or JavaScript — an id.
blocks.19.items.3: An id rule beats a class rule, whatever the order.
blocks.20.text: One to keep in mind: `#hours` and `[id="hours"]` match exactly the same element, but they don't weigh the same in an argument. You'll meet the second in lesson 12 and the weight in lesson 50. And here too the name has to start with a letter: `id="123item"` is legal HTML, but `#123item` in CSS doesn't work.
walkthrough.0.text:
Both attributes sit in the same tag: `

`. Their order doesn't matter. The `id` stayed because the link at the top uses it — remove it and the jump stops working, and nothing tells you.
walkthrough.1.text: I deleted `#hours { }` rather than reworking it. The temptation is to leave it "just in case" — it works and nothing breaks. But two records of the same thing drift apart at the first change, and since an id beats a class, the one that wins is precisely the one you forgot.
walkthrough.2.text: I didn't touch `.note` at all. It arrived finished from lesson 09 and was already doing the whole job — it was only missing a second element. Sometimes the fix is to add a label, not to write a rule.
walkthrough.3.text: Notice what happened to the count: two notes, one rule. Tomorrow there are five — the rule isn't touched. That's exactly what `id` can't give you, and exactly why classes are what styling uses.
